section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Introduction
paragraphs.text: The Council for Media Services (CMS) has competencies arising from the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD) and the Digital Services Act (DSA), which are embedded in the Law on Media Services (ZMS). Since the measures emphasized in the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act are closely linked to these legislative frameworks, CMS is contributing into the consultation process. CMS welcomes measures that aim to strengthen consumer protection and increase safety in the online environment, especially in the context of protecting minors.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Introduction
paragraphs.text: The measures announced in the public consultation build on the European Commission's so-called "Fitness Check"⁵ analysis from 2024, which identifies problematic practices, analyses legal frameworks, and highlights legislative gaps in the area of consumer protection in the digital space. As the analysis itself demonstrated, most of these practices are already covered by existing legislation. CMS agrees with the identified gaps but sees room for adjusting existing regulatory frameworks or for developing supplementary guidelines to the articles.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Introduction
paragraphs.text: Such an opportunity will be provided, for example, by the upcoming evaluation and potential revision of the AVMSD, as well as the evaluation of the DSA planned for 2027. CMS considers this approach more effective not only in terms of a transparent legislative environment but also in terms of the speed of applying changes in practice. The DFA proposal is expected in 2026, and historical experience shows that the process of adopting a new framework may take 2–3 years.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: The regulatory practice of the CMS, as coordinator of digital services, shows that the full potential of the DSA has not yet been carried out. Currently, CMS is in the initial phase of

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: supervision, finalising the process of identifying and categorizing entities in Slovakia regulated under the DSA. Based on the results of this activity, it will then continue with active enforcement of its provisions. Since many of the measures announced in the DFA overlap with the DSA, the CMS sees considerable potential to address them precisely through the provisions of the DSA. More detailed clarifications and instructions for their application can be additionally defined in guidelines for individual articles. $^{6}$, which detail possible measures to safeguard them from digital risks. Article 22 guidelines are currently under development, and further guidelines, including those for Article 25, are anticipated.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: Dark patterns that pressure and manipulate consumers are broadly defined in the DSA. They are regulated by:

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: Article 25 on the design and organization of online interfaces, which prohibits operating online interfaces in a way that misleads or manipulates users;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: Article 34 on risk assessment, requiring very large online platforms (VLOPs) and very large online search engines (VLOSEs) to analyze systemic risks arising from the operation of their services;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: Article 35 on risk mitigation, which obliges them to implement effective measures to mitigate identified systemic risks.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: Dark patterns are also explicitly mentioned in Recital 67 of the DSA, where such practices are defined and prohibited. In the guidelines on Article 28 of the DSA, section 6.6 on commercial practices, the European Commission recommends specific measures to protect minors from dark patterns.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check analysis identified a gap in Article 25 of the DSA, which explicitly excludes practices covered by the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) $^{7}$. The analysis further claims that given the scope of the UCPD, which covers almost all business-to-consumer (B2C) practices, the DSA will likely have only limited relevance in regulating dark patterns in B2C. The analysis also noted that Article 25 applies only to online platforms and thus does not include small and micro-enterprises, search engines, traders who do not qualify as intermediaries, or digital services whose platform elements are only complementary. CMS, however, sees room to address these legislative gaps either by supplementing the DSA (in case of a future revision) or through guidelines regarding the scope of included entities, since the regulation already provides a basis for addressing dark patterns.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: The issue of unfair personalization practices exploiting consumer vulnerability is addressed by Article 26 of the DSA on advertising on online platforms. This article prohibits platforms from presenting users with personalized advertising based on special categories of data defined in Articles 4 and 9 of the GDPR, such as data on health, economic situation or interests. Article 28 of the DSA prohibits personalized advertising towards minors. The guidelines on Article 28, section 6.5.1 on recommender systems for minors, propose concrete measures. In point c), the Commission recommends that such systems should not use behavioural data of minors obtained from their activity on the platform. The issue of unfair personalization practices can also be addressed through Articles 34 and 35 of the DSA.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check analysis points out gaps in the scope of Article 26 of the DSA, which covers only online platforms, thereby excluding other actors engaged in personalized advertising. Again, CMS sees room here for supplementing the DSA in a future revision or through guidelines, since the regulation already sets rules in the context of personalisation practices.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: The Commission in the public consultation also mentions the problem of misleading marketing practices by influencers. Article 26 of the DSA on advertising on online platforms regulates the functionality of ad identification. According to this article, platform providers must ensure that users can clearly identify advertising content. It also sets rules for providers of online platforms presenting advertisements on their interfaces to ensure that users of the services can identify them as commercial content.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: DSA
paragraphs.text: The Commission argues in the Fitness Check analysis that the DSA does not cover all cases of influencer marketing, and only covers advertising based on remuneration to be provided to the platform. In this case, CMS sees the benefit of Article 9(a) AVMSD, which requires influencers registered as providers of audiovisual media service (AVMS) to make any commercial communication recognizable. Unlike the DSA, it covers all commercial communication published by AVMS providers. CMS considers that this AVMSD article sufficiently covers the gap identified in the European Commission's analysis.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: There is also room to enforce the measures announced in the public consultation under the AVMSD. The experience of CMS shows that the DSA, which regulates the infrastructure and procedures of intermediary services, and the AVMSD, which is more content-oriented, complement each other well. Together, they represent a strong tool to address measures that should be covered in the DFA.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: In addition to insufficient labelling of influencer advertising, the European Commission also pointed in its consultation document to the issue of promoting harmful products. However, the AVMSD has a strong position in regulating audiovisual media service providers. The AVMSD specifically regulates this in:

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Article 9(a) – requiring commercial communication to be clearly distinguishable from other content;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Article 10 – imposing an obligation to disclose sponsored programs;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Articles 9(d), (e), (f) – prohibiting or restricting commercial communication for products such as tobacco, alcohol, or prescription medicines;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Article 28b(a) – requiring video-sharing platforms (VSPs) to take measures to protect minors from inappropriate commercial communication.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: CMS agrees with the Commission’s statement in the Fitness Check analysis that the AVMSD remains the main legislative framework for influencer marketing practices. In the context of a potential revision of the AVMSD, greater harmonization in regulating AVMS providers across the EU could be beneficial. As noted in the 2022 ERGA report, EU countries regulate them with different approaches. In addition, in many countries self-regulatory mechanisms have introduced their own rules on commercial communication aimed at AVMS providers. An example of harmonizing elements within the AVMSD could be the threshold criteria used to determine who falls within the regulatory framework.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Unfair personalization practices are also regulated by the AVMSD. The Directive addresses these practices primarily through:

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Article 6a, which emphasizes that personal data of minors collected by media service providers must not be processed for commercial purposes;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Article 28b(3), which prohibits VSPs from using minors’ data for commercial purposes.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: CMS agrees with the Fitness Check analysis, which highlights the relevance of the AVMSD in the area of unfair personalization. Together with the DSA, it provides tools to address this issue.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: Practices such as dark patterns in commercial communication are also regulated by the AVMSD. The Directive sets rules for AVMS and VSP providers with respect to commercial communication. These are set out in Article 9, specifically:

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: (1)(a) prohibiting surreptitious commercial communication;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: (1)(b) prohibiting subliminal techniques;

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: RADA PRE MEDIALE SLUŽBY

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: (1)(g) prohibiting the exploitation of minors' inexperience to encourage purchase of a product or service.

section_path: Contribution of the Council for Media Services for the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act (DFA)
Potential to address issues through existing legislation: AVMSD
paragraphs.text: For practices linked to dark patterns, the AVMSD supplements Article 25 of the DSA, which covers online platforms.

section_path: Conclusion
paragraphs.text: The Council for Media Services recognizes the importance of strengthening consumer protection in the digital environment, particularly regarding the protection of minors. However, CMS believes that the issues identified in the public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act can be effectively addressed through existing legislative frameworks, the Digital Services Act and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive.

section_path: Conclusion
paragraphs.text: The DSA and AVMSD already contain robust provisions covering dark patterns, unfair personalization practices, influencer marketing, and protection of minors. Where legislative gaps exist, these can be addressed more efficiently through targeted amendments during the upcoming AVMSD evaluation and the planned 2027 DSA review, or through detailed implementation guidelines currently being developed.

section_path: Conclusion
paragraphs.text: CMS advocates for prioritizing the full implementation and enforcement of existing frameworks rather than creating new legislation. This approach offers greater regulatory coherence, faster implementation in practice, and a more transparent legislative environment. Given that the DFA proposal is expected in 2026 with an adoption process potentially extending 2–3 years, leveraging existing regulatory frameworks represents a more pragmatic path to achieving consumer protection goals sooner.

section_path: Conclusion
paragraphs.text: CMS remains committed to effective supervision and enforcement of both the DSA and AVMSD provisions and stands ready to contribute to future evaluations and revisions of these frameworks.
